title: Setting up a wormery from a stacking bin
subtitle: Tiger worms, bedding, and a feed routine for worm casting production
excerpt: A wormery uses composting worms (Eisenia fetida, not garden earthworms) to convert food scraps into fine worm castings and a liquid feed within 8-12 weeks. A two-tray stacking bin handles roughly 2 kg of food waste per week. Worms, bedding, and a tray cost around to start.
sourceNotes: Wormcity and Original Organics wormery setup guides. WRAP composting alternatives. Original research: Edwards and Bohlen, Biology and Ecology of Earthworms (1996), tiger worm temperature and feed rate data.

glossaryTerms.definition: A composting system using composting worms (typically Eisenia fetida — tiger worms or red wigglers) to break down food waste into fine-textured worm castings. Distinct from garden earthworms, which cannot be used.
The liquid that drains from the wormery. Worm leachate is dark and nutrient-rich. Dilute 1:10 with water before use as a liquid plant feed.

content.text: processes kitchen food scraps faster per volume than a standard compost bin, and produces two outputs: worm castings (fine, dense compost) and a liquid feed. The key condition is temperature, tiger worms are most active between 15°C and 25°C. Below 5°C they slow to a near stop; above 30°C they die.

attrs.title: Siting the wormery
attrs.body: Keep it in a sheltered, frost-free location: a shed, garage, or outdoor store in winter; any shaded spot in summer. Direct sun raises the bin temperature rapidly and can kill the worms.

content.text: Fill the first tray 50 mm deep with damp bedding, coir (coconut fibre), torn and moistened cardboard, or shredded newspaper. The bedding should be wet enough to hold its shape when squeezed but not drip. Add the worms on top and allow them to migrate down into the bedding before adding food.

Add a thin layer (20-30 mm) of food waste: vegetable peelings, fruit scraps, tea bags, coffee grounds, crushed eggshells. Avoid onion, citrus, and acidic foods in large quantities, they slow the worms. Cover each food addition with a thin layer of torn cardboard or damp newspaper. This prevents flies and keeps the surface aerobic.
Most stacking bins have a sump tray and drain tap at the base. Drain
weekly. Dilute 1:10 for use as a liquid feed on established plants.

After 8-12 weeks the lower tray will be dense with dark castings and most food residue will have gone. Stop feeding the lower tray, place fresh bedding and food in the upper tray, and allow the worms to migrate upward over two to four weeks. Then empty the lower tray of castings, most worms will have moved up.

items.cause: Bin too acidic, too wet, or newly set up (worms explore their boundaries in the first 48 hours)
items.fix: After the first 48 hours: check pH by adding crushed eggshells or lime powder. Check the bedding is damp, not soaked.
Cover every food addition with a layer of cardboard or damp newspaper. Reduce fruit in the feed if persistent.

items.cause: Temperature outside 5-30°C range, or bin waterlogged
items.fix: Move to a sheltered location. Check drainage is clear. Add dry torn cardboard to absorb excess moisture.
